segments.text: Neuronal Signaling and Pharmacodynamics

Ligand binding and neurosignaling
Neurotransmitters:
Small molecule ligands that mediate signaling
Bind to cell surface receptor, trigger desired effect
Communication between neurons or from neuron to “effector organ”

Neurotransmitters
A neurotransmitter is a chemical messenger that transmits nerve impulses from one neuron to another.
The space between the two neurons is called a synapse (AKA synaptic cleft) .
The presynaptic neuron releases the neuro -transmitter.
The postsynaptic neuron contains the receptors that bind the neurotransmitter.

Neurotransmitters
Once the neurotransmitter is bound to the receptor, the chemical message is delivered.
The transmitter is then degraded or returned to the presynaptic neuron to begin the process again.

Some Famous Neurotransmitters
Acetylcholine
Serotonin
Dopamine
Norepinephrine
Histamine
Epinephrine (adrenaline):
Not a neurotransmitter (it is a hormone)
Secreted by the adrenal gland
Affects nerve function
Neurotransmitter: released from a neuron
Hormone: released from a gland

Neurotransmitters : Acetylcholine
Acetylcholine is a quaternary ammonium ion
that serves as a neurotransmitter between neurons
and muscle cells .
Effect: excitation (nerves) or contraction (in muscles)
Activates cholinergic receptors

Acetylcholine degradation
Acetylcholine is quickly degraded by the enzyme acetylcholinesterase
Acetylcholinesterase serves a very important function: too much acetylcholine =  nerves/muscles unable to rebound from activation

Acetylcholinesterase mechanism
Acetylcholinesterase
active site contains a reactive serine that carries out neucleophilic attack on the carbonyl.
Histidine is important in removing H from serine -OH
Can you guess the mechanism?

Neurotransmitters : Agonists & Antagonists
Antagonists : Molecule that interferes with normal neurotransmitter signaling:
Prevent the release of a neurotransmitter
Or block its binding to a  receptor
Agonists: Molecule that enhances neurotransmitter signaling:
Molecules that mimic the effect of neutrotransmitter
Or increase the amount of neuro -transmitter released
Or block its degradation

Acetylcholine Antagonists
Atropine is isolated from the Atropa belladonna , or deadly nightshade plant.
It relaxes smooth muscle and interferes with nerve impulses, and is toxic in  high doses .
Botulinum toxin:
Blocks release of Acetylcholine from secretion vesicles
Result: paralysis
Clinical use: Botox
Works by blocking acetylcholne receptor

Acetylcholine agonists
Organophosphates:
Pesticides
Nerve agents: sarin
Mechanism:
Block the breakdown of acetylcholine
Suicide Inhibitors of acetylcholineasterases
Acetylcholine is not degraded, signal persists
Symptoms: Excitation, sweating, urination, salivation, diarrhea, death
Treatment: Atropine
Sarin (Nerve agent)

Sarin poisoning
How does sarin inhibit acetylcholinesterase ?
Sarin acts as a suicide inhibitor of acetylcholinesterase
Can you propose a mechanism?
Sarin (Nerve agent)

18.8	Neurotransmitters C. Acetylcholine and Nicotine Addiction
Nicotine affects acetylcholine (cholinergic)
Receptors between neurons but not between neurons
and muscle cells .

18.8	Neurotransmitters C. Acetylcholine and Nicotine Addiction
At low concentrations, nicotine binds to acetyl-choline receptors (agonist), resulting in feelings of wellbeing and alertness .
This triggers other nerve cells to release dopamine , which increases the pleasure sensation.
A person becomes addicted to the pleasure sensation gained from taking in nicotine.
As the dopamine receptors deplete over time, a person will crave more nicotine to reproduce the “ high ” feelings.

18.8	Neurotransmitters A. Norepinephrine and Dopamine
Dopamine is a derivative of an amino acid, can you guess which one?
Tyrosine

Neurotransmitters Serotonin
Serotonin is synthesized from an amino acid,
Which one?
Tryptophan

18.8	Neurotransmitters B. Serotonin
Serotonin is important in mood, sleep, perception, and temperature regulation.
A deficiency of serotonin causes depression , so many antidepressant drugs are selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs).
The drugs inhibit the reuptake of serotonin by the presynaptic neuron, effectively increasing its concentration .

18.8	Neurotransmitters B. Serotonin
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs).
The drugs inhibit the reuptake of serotonin by the presynaptic neuron, effectively increasing its concentration .
Are SSRIs Agonists or Antagonists of serotonin?
Agonists: they increase the effect of serotonin

18.8	Neurotransmitters B. Serotonin
Examples of antidepressant drugs are shown
below:
Both are types of SSRI drugs

Neurotransmitters Serotonin
Compounds bufotenin and psilocin are very
similar in structure to serotonin, and they both
cause intense hallucinations .
LSD

Ligand specificity and response
So, how do the different ligands which bind to the same receptor produce different responses?
Answer: for many receptors, there are more than just and “on” or “off” conformation
This is especially true for GPCRs, but RTKs are also found to have multiple conformations in response to different ligands
Example: serotonin receptors

Serotonin receptor
There are several types of serotonin receptors
Example: 5H2-2B receptor
GPCR, binds to a Gq G-protein
Binds many molecules including serotonin & LSD
Unlike serotonin, LSD has a much higher affinity and longer lasting effect
Ligand 1
Response 1
Ligand 2
Response 2
Serotonin
Sleep, mood
Short lived
LSD
Hallucinations
Long lived

LSD and serotonin receptor
LSD has higher affinity:
Slower dissociation time from the receptor than serotonin
LSD interacts with more side chains than serotonin
Structure reveals a “lid” that keeps LSD bound for longer than serotonin

LSD and serotonin receptor
The so-called EL2 lid explains why LSD has a slower dissociation time  higher affinity  longer lasting effect
But how is the effect of LSD different from serotonin?
Different signaling pathway

Different ligand, different response
Calcium release is observed in serotonin signaling, but much less in LSD signaling
Serotonin
Gq activation
IP3 (2 nd messenger)
Calcium release
LSD
Hallucination
Sleep, mood regulation
Gq activation
IP3 (2 nd messenger)
No Calcium release

Functional selectivity
“Whereas most endogenous agonists, such as serotonin, activate both G-protein and [other] pathways, some compounds can stabilize distinct receptor conformations, thereby preferentially activating select signal transduction pathways. This phenomenon has been termed ‘functional selectivity’ or ‘biased agonism ’ ”
from: Cell 168, 377–389, January 26, 2017
Instead of 2 states: (ligand-free: off and ligand-bound: on), there are multiple degrees of activation of a receptor
Each ligand induces a slightly different conformation, altering the interaction of the receptor with the intracellular components, resulting in drastically different physiological outputs

The plot thickens: olfactory receptors
Olfactory (smell) receptors:
A few hundred GPCRs
How can a limited number of receptors distinguish between a vast number of odors?
Key: specificity and cross reactivity
Each receptor can bind a large number of molecules with different affinities
Each molecule can bind to a number of receptors with different affinities
Result: multiple neurons triggered at different degrees in response for each odor

Olfactory sensing
Recommended reading: review article on olfactory sensors: Cell, 2009, 193, p45 [http://www.cell.com/cell/pdf/S0092-8674(09)01175-1.pdf]

Olfactory sensing
Additionally, different receptors have different affinities for the same odor molecule
At low concentration, the odor only triggers a few receptors
At high concentrations, more receptors are triggered
The same molecule can be perceived differently at different concentrations!
Humans: ~900 ORs, ~60% are pseudogenes
~ 9 million expressed in the olfactory bulb
Dogs: ~1500 ORs, ~18% are pseudogenes
~250 million expressed in the olfactory bulb
